title: Evaluate Reverse Polish Notation
description: You are given an array of strings tokens that represents an arithmetic expression in Reverse Polish Notation. Evaluate the expression and return an integer that represents the value of the expression. Valid operators are '+', '-', '*', and '/'. Each operand may be an integer or another expression. Division between two integers always truncates toward zero. There will not be any division by zero. The input represents a valid arithmetic expression in RPN. The answer and all intermediate calculations can be represented in a 32-bit integer.
constraints: tokens[i] is either an operator '+', '-', '*', or '/', or an integer in the range [-200, 200]
thinking_questions: In RPN, operators always apply to the two most recently seen operands â€” what data structure gives you 'most recently seen'?
When you see an operator, how many operands do you need and where are they?
What does integer division truncating toward zero mean? How do you implement that in Python?

hints.L1: In RPN an operator always applies to the two most recently encountered numbers â€” think about which structure gives you constant-time access to the most recent items.
hints.L2: Stack. Push numbers. When you see an operator, pop two numbers, apply the operator, push the result. Final answer is the only remaining element.

follow_up_questions: Why does pop order matter â€” why is `b, a = stack.pop(), stack.pop()` correct?
What does 'truncate toward zero' mean for division, and how does Python's // differ?
How would you convert an infix expression to RPN? (Shunting-yard algorithm)
solution_notes: Stack: push numbers, operators pop two and push result. Watch pop order for subtraction/division. int(a/b) for truncate-toward-zero.
